name: Cover Seeking
description: An AI positioning behavior where an agent chooses and moves to cover based on threats, sightlines, reservations, and attack options.
design_notes: Cover seeking is a spatial decision, not a simple destination pick. It needs cover_slot_id, target_id, exposure score, reservation rules, path cost, peek points, and commitment windows so agents do not shuffle endlessly between similar options.
design_purpose: Make combatants use the environment defensively and create readable tactical spaces.
Encourage flanking, grenades, suppression, or movement tools by making cover meaningful.
Let designers control encounter difficulty through cover availability and quality.
player_fantasy: The player feels like enemies are tactically aware and can be outmaneuvered by changing angles or pressure.

parameters.name: cover_slot_id
parameters.description: Stable identifier for a usable cover position or side.

parameters.name: exposure_score_threshold
parameters.description: Maximum allowed exposure to active target_id before a slot is considered unsafe.

parameters.name: cover_reservation_ms
parameters.description: Time a chosen slot is reserved to prevent multiple agents choosing the same point.

parameters.name: peek_exposure_interval_ms
parameters.description: Minimum interval between exposing the agent for attacks or observation.

parameters.name: cover_repath_hysteresis
parameters.description: Score margin required before abandoning current cover for a better slot.

edge_cases: All nearby cover slots are reserved, unreachable, or exposed to a second target.
Destructible cover disappears while the agent is entering a cover animation.
Tall and low cover require different stance, aim, and hitbox synchronization.
A networked game needs server authority over cover reservations to prevent overlap.
common_bugs: Agents choose cover based only on distance and stand on the player's side of the wall.
cover_slot_id reservations never expire after an agent dies or is interrupted.
Cover animation snaps the character out of navmesh position and breaks line-of-sight traces.
Debug tools show cover points but not exposure_score, reservation owner, or rejection reason.
balancing_notes: High-quality cover makes enemies survivable; pair it with flanking routes or destructible counters so fights do not stall.
Too much cover seeking can reduce aggression unless some enemies prefer push, flank, or suppress actions.
Reservation and hysteresis settings shape whether squads look coordinated or indecisive.
accessibility_notes: Use readable silhouettes, barks, or posture changes so players can tell when an enemy is in cover or preparing to peek.
Avoid making tiny exposed limbs the only viable target unless aim assists or alternate tactics exist.

implementation_notes.unity: Represent cover slots as baked points or runtime probes with cover_slot_id, normal, stance, and peek anchors; score slots against target_id and draw rejection reasons in gizmos.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Area nodes or generated cover Resources with reservation state; NavigationAgent movement should coordinate with animation states for entering, holding, and leaving cover.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use EQS for cover candidate scoring, Blackboard keys for CoverSlot and TargetActor, and GameplayTasks/StateTree to coordinate movement, crouch, and peek animations.
implementation_notes.web: Precompute or sample cover cells, reserve chosen slots by agent ID, and log score components so tactical behavior can be replayed in deterministic debug sessions.

example_games.title: Gears of War
example_games.note: Uses cover positioning as a central combat affordance.

example_games.title: XCOM
example_games.note: Uses cover quality as a major tactical consideration.
